title: 08 - Environmental and Nutritional Diseases - Exam Prep Questions
description: Third-party generated single-choice exam-prep questions for Chapter 8. The set is aligned primarily to the Chapter 8 textbook content and shaped to reflect the style, tone, and difficulty distribution of the RCPA Basic Pathological Sciences mock exam format. Choices are keyed so answers remain stable if an application randomizes display order.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These questions are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.answer_key_note: Answers are included as stable choice keys with brief source-grounded rationales for study and review purposes.

source_notes.reference_note: Used as a style, structure, and difficulty reference.

questions.question:
A coastal population experiences recurrent heat waves, more severe flooding, and changing mosquito habitats after a sustained rise in greenhouse gases.

Which health effect is MOST directly predicted by these changes?

choices.text: Lower risk of malnutrition because crop yields rise
Increased water-borne infection and vector-borne disease burden
Reduced cardiopulmonary stress because ozone levels fall

answer.reason: The chapter links climate change to flooding-related water contamination, changes in vector distribution, and broader increases in infectious disease burden.

questions.question:
A solvent is converted by hepatic cytochrome P-450 into a reactive free radical that damages cell membranes.

This is BEST described as which process?

choices.text: Phase I metabolic activation of a xenobiotic
Phase II conjugation to a nontoxic water-soluble product
Receptor antagonism at the site of entry

answer.reason: Phase I reactions mediated by cytochrome P-450 can detoxify xenobiotics or activate them into reactive toxic intermediates.

questions.question: Which component of outdoor air pollution is MOST likely to reach the alveoli and trigger macrophage-mediated inflammation?

choices.text: Particles less than 10 micrometers in diameter

answer.reason: Fine particulates under 10 micrometers travel to the alveoli, where they are phagocytosed and incite inflammation.

questions.question:
A man is found unconscious in a closed garage with a running car engine.

What is the PRIMARY mechanism of tissue hypoxia in this setting?

answer.reason: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with very high affinity and forms carboxyhemoglobin, preventing effective oxygen transport.

questions.question:
A 4-year-old child from an old house has learning difficulty, abdominal pain, microcytic anemia, and basophilic stippling of erythrocytes.

Which additional biochemical abnormality is MOST likely?

choices.text: Isolated folate depletion with normal heme synthesis
Inhibition of aminolevulinic acid dehydratase and ferrochelatase

answer.reason: Lead inhibits key heme-synthesis enzymes, producing microcytic anemia, basophilic stippling, and accumulation of zinc-protoporphyrin-related markers.

questions.question:
A pregnant woman regularly eats large predatory fish from contaminated waters. Her infant is later born with severe neurologic impairment.

Which disorder BEST fits this exposure pattern?

answer.reason: Fetal methyl mercury exposure from contaminated fish can produce Minamata disease, with severe CNS damage in the developing brain.

questions.question:
A rural population using arsenic-contaminated groundwater develops palmoplantar hyperkeratosis, patchy skin hyperpigmentation, and multiple skin cancers.

What is the MOST important underlying toxic action of arsenic in acute poisoning?

answer.reason: Acute arsenic toxicity is strongly linked to interference with oxidative phosphorylation, while chronic exposure causes distinctive skin and cancer changes.

questions.question: Which occupational exposure is classically associated with angiosarcoma of the liver?

answer.reason: Vinyl chloride exposure is a classic cause of hepatic angiosarcoma.

questions.question: Which statement about tobacco is MOST accurate?

choices.text: It is mainly associated with renal cell carcinoma but not lung cancer
It is the most common exogenous cause of human cancer
It causes dependence only when smoked, not when chewed
It lowers the risk of myocardial infarction in heavy users
Passive smoke has no measurable clinical effect in nonsmokers

answer.reason: Tobacco is the most common exogenous cause of human cancer and is responsible for the great majority of lung cancers.

questions.question:
A 58-year-old man who both smokes heavily and drinks heavily develops a laryngeal squamous carcinoma.

Which principle BEST explains this association?

choices.text: Alcohol fully protects against tobacco-related mutation
Laryngeal cancers are unrelated to either exposure
Nicotine is the sole carcinogen in cigarette smoke
Tobacco and alcohol have multiplicative carcinogenic effects on the upper aerodigestive tract

answer.reason: Smoking and alcohol together multiply the risk of oral, laryngeal, and esophageal cancers.

questions.question: At usual blood alcohol concentrations, which enzyme is primarily responsible for ethanol oxidation in hepatocyte cytosol?

answer.reason: At usual intake, ethanol is metabolized mainly in hepatocyte cytosol by alcohol dehydrogenase.

questions.question:
A chronic alcoholic develops fatty change in the liver.

What metabolic disturbance MOST directly contributes to this lesion?

choices.text: Increased NADH/NAD+ ratio inhibiting fatty-acid oxidation

answer.reason: Alcohol metabolism raises the NADH/NAD+ ratio, suppressing fatty-acid oxidation and promoting hepatic steatosis.

questions.question:
A patient takes a large intentional overdose of acetaminophen and then develops jaundice and hepatic failure.

Which pathologic lesion is MOST characteristic?

choices.text: Centrilobular hepatic necrosis due to NAPQI accumulation

answer.reason: Acetaminophen overdose generates excess NAPQI, depletes glutathione, and produces centrilobular hepatic necrosis.

questions.question:
An older patient taking high-dose aspirin for chronic inflammatory pain develops epigastric pain, bruising, and gastrointestinal bleeding.

Which mechanism BEST accounts for the bleeding tendency?

choices.text: Irreversible inhibition of platelet thromboxane A2 synthesis
Activation of protein C by aspirin

answer.reason: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase and blocks thromboxane A2 production, impairing platelet aggregation.

questions.question:
A 29-year-old man develops crushing chest pain shortly after using cocaine.

Which mechanism MOST directly explains cocaine-associated myocardial ischemia?

choices.text: Coronary vasoconstriction with enhanced platelet aggregation

answer.reason: Cocaine increases catecholamine signaling, causes coronary vasospasm, promotes platelet aggregation, and can precipitate myocardial infarction.

questions.question:
An intravenous heroin user is admitted with fever and multiple septic pulmonary emboli.

Which cardiac site is MOST likely infected?

answer.reason: Intravenous heroin use is classically associated with right-sided infective endocarditis, especially involving the tricuspid valve.

questions.question:
A patient sustains burns over 35% of total body surface area.

What is the MOST important early systemic consequence to anticipate?

choices.text: Rapid fluid shift into interstitial compartments causing hypovolemic shock

answer.reason: Major burns cause rapid fluid movement into interstitium, producing hypovolemic shock early in the course.

questions.question:
During a summer race, a runner collapses with hot dry skin and a core temperature of 106 F.

Which diagnosis is MOST likely?

answer.reason: Heat stroke reflects failure of thermoregulation with marked hyperthermia and high mortality, unlike heat exhaustion where temperature regulation is preserved.

questions.question: Why can ordinary household alternating current be particularly dangerous when a live wire is grasped?

choices.text: It induces tetanic muscle spasm that prolongs contact

answer.reason: Alternating current can trigger tetanic spasm, locking the victim onto the source and prolonging current flow.

questions.question: Which radiation unit adjusts the absorbed dose for biologic effectiveness and radiation type?

answer.reason: The sievert expresses equivalent dose and corrects absorbed dose for biologic effectiveness of the radiation type.

questions.question:
After a whole-body radiation exposure of 5 Sv, a patient develops profound neutropenia and thrombocytopenia over the next 1 to 2 weeks.

Which tissue has suffered the dominant injury?

answer.reason: At this dose range, the dominant acute radiation syndrome reflects marrow aplasia with severe cytopenias.

questions.question:
A severely undernourished child has marked loss of subcutaneous fat and muscle, no major edema, and only mildly reduced serum albumin.

Which diagnosis is MOST likely?

answer.reason: Marasmus reflects severe calorie deficiency with muscle and fat wasting and relatively preserved visceral protein stores.

questions.question:
A child with relatively preserved calorie intake but severe protein deficiency presents with edema, fatty liver, and skin and hair changes.

Which diagnosis is MOST likely?

answer.reason: Kwashiorkor is the edematous severe acute malnutrition syndrome caused by relative protein deficiency with visceral protein depletion.

questions.question:
A young woman with self-induced vomiting has palpitations and collapses suddenly.

Which metabolic abnormality is the MOST likely immediate contributor to this event?

answer.reason: Bulimia and anorexia can both produce hypokalemia, which increases the risk of lethal cardiac arrhythmias.

questions.question:
A child with vitamin A deficiency first notices poor vision at dusk and later develops xerophthalmia.

Which process is MOST directly impaired by this deficiency?

choices.text: Mineralization of osteoid by alkaline phosphatase only
Visual pigment function and specialized epithelial differentiation

answer.reason: Vitamin A is required for normal visual pigment function and maintenance of specialized epithelia, explaining night blindness and xerophthalmia.

questions.question:
A patient with chronic vitamin D deficiency has near-normal serum calcium but persistent hypophosphatemia.

What BEST explains the low phosphate level?

choices.text: Vitamin D directly increases phosphate wasting in the bowel
Secondary hyperparathyroidism increases renal phosphate wasting

answer.reason: Vitamin D deficiency stimulates parathyroid hormone secretion, which helps normalize calcium but drives renal phosphate wasting.

questions.question:
A patient with perifollicular hemorrhages, swollen gums, and poor wound healing is found to have vitamin C deficiency.

What biochemical defect MOST directly underlies these findings?

choices.text: Inability to activate vitamin K-dependent clotting proteins
Impaired hydroxylation of collagen proline and lysine residues

answer.reason: Vitamin C is required for hydroxylation reactions during collagen synthesis, so deficiency weakens vessel walls and impairs wound repair.

questions.question: Which pattern of adipose tissue distribution carries the greatest metabolic risk?

answer.reason: The chapter emphasizes that central or visceral obesity carries a far higher risk for metabolic syndrome and systemic disease than diffuse subcutaneous fat.

questions.question: Which adipocyte-derived hormone normally suppresses food intake by stimulating POMC/CART neurons and inhibiting NPY/AgRP neurons?

answer.reason: Leptin is the main adiposity signal from fat stores and normally acts through the hypothalamus to reduce food intake and increase energy expenditure.

questions.question: In obesity-associated carcinogenesis, hyperinsulinemia is thought to increase the bioavailability of which mitogenic signal?

answer.reason: One proposed link between obesity and cancer is hyperinsulinemia with increased free IGF-1 signaling, which promotes growth pathways in susceptible cells.
